senses.definition: A very rare or likely incorrect form in English, with no standard meaning.
examples: The word "anstosse" does not have a standard meaning in English.
Most dictionaries do not list "anstosse" as an English word.

senses.definition: A given name used in some cultures; it has no specific meaning in general English.
examples: Ansu arrived early for the meeting.
I think Ansu will enjoy this book.

senses.definition: A very rare or likely incorrect form in English, with no standard meaning.
examples: The verb "ansulate" is not used in standard English.
Learners should avoid using "ansulate" because it is not recognized.

senses.definition: Something you say or write when someone asks you a question.
examples: Her answer was short and clear.
Please write your answers on the sheet of paper.

senses.definition: The correct result or information in a test or problem.
examples: Do you know the answer to question twelve?
The answers are at the back of the book.

senses.definition: A way of solving a problem or dealing with a difficult situation.
examples: More money is not always the answer to every problem.
Working longer hours may seem like the answer, but it is not.

senses.definition: To speak or write back to someone who has asked you something.
examples: She asked a question but he did not answer.
Please answer my email as soon as you can.

senses.definition: To pick up the phone or open the door when it rings or someone knocks.
examples: Can you answer the phone, please?
She ran to answer the door.

senses.definition: To give a solution or reaction to a problem or situation.
examples: This plan does not answer all our needs.
The new policy answers some of the concerns people had.

senses.definition: Plural of answerability; different kinds or cases of being responsible for something.
examples: Different legal answerabilities may arise from the same action.
The report examines the various answerabilities of public officials.

senses.definition: The state of having to explain or accept responsibility for what you do.
examples: In a democracy, answerability of leaders is essential.
The new rules increase the answerability of managers to the public.

senses.definition: Having to explain your actions to someone in authority; responsible to someone.
examples: Ministers are answerable to parliament for their decisions.
As project leader, you are answerable for the final result.

senses.definition: That can be answered.
examples: The question is difficult but answerable.
Most of these problems are answerable with the data we have.

senses.definition: The quality of being responsible for something or of being able to be answered.
examples: They discussed the moral answerableness of their choices.
The answerableness of the question depends on the available evidence.

senses.definition: In a way that shows responsibility or can be explained.
examples: Officials must act answerably to the law and to the public.
Resources should be used answerably and transparently.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of answer: said or wrote something back to someone who asked something.
examples: He answered quickly and confidently.
She answered my letter the next day.

senses.definition: A person who gives an answer, especially in a survey, test, or discussion.
examples: Each answerer was given two minutes to respond.
If the answerer is unsure, they may skip the question.

senses.definition: Plural of answerer; people who give answers, especially in tests or surveys.
examples: Most answerers chose the first option.
The survey answerers were between 18 and 25 years old.

senses.definition: Present participle of answer: saying or writing something back to someone who asks something.
examples: She is busy answering emails.
He kept answering without thinking.

senses.definition: In a way that gives an answer; in response.
examples: He nodded answeringly, without saying a word.
She smiled answeringly when he called her name.

senses.definition: Having no answer; not answered or impossible to answer.
examples: He stared at the answerless question on the page.
Some of life's biggest questions remain answerless.

senses.definition: In a way that gives no answer.
examples: She gazed answerlessly at the empty screen.
He shrugged answerlessly and walked away.

senses.definition: A machine attached to a telephone that records messages when you are not there.
examples: I left a message on their answerphone.
The answerphone was full, so I could not record my message.
collocations: leave a message on the answerphone

senses.definition: Plural of answerphone; machines that record telephone messages when people are not there.
examples: Many homes used answerphones before voicemail became common.
Both answerphones were already full of messages.

senses.definition: Plural of answer: things you say or write when people ask you questions.
examples: His answers were honest and direct.
Please check your answers before you hand in the test.

senses.definition: Third person singular of answer: says or writes something back to someone who asks something.
examples: If anyone calls, she answers immediately.
He never answers when I text him.

senses.definition: A very small insect that lives in large groups, often in the ground.
examples: An ant crawled across the kitchen floor.
There is an ant nest in the garden.

senses.definition: In architecture, a small square post or pillar at the end of a wall, often found beside columns.
examples: The ancient temple was decorated with columns and antae.
An anta can frame the side of a doorway in classical buildings.

senses.definition: A medicine that reduces or stops the burning feeling of too much acid in the stomach.
examples: She took an antacid for her heartburn.
Many people use antacids after a heavy meal.

senses.definition: Able to reduce or prevent stomach acid.
examples: The antacid effect of the drug is very fast.
These tablets have strong antacid properties.

senses.definition: Plural of antacid; medicines that reduce or stop the burning feeling of too much acid in the stomach.
examples: He keeps antacids in his bag for his stomach problems.
Doctors sometimes recommend antacids for mild heartburn.

senses.definition: A very rare or technical word in English, with no common general meaning.
examples: The term "antacrid" is not used in everyday English.
Most learners will never meet the word "antacrid".

senses.definition: A very rare or technical word in English, with no common general meaning.
examples: The adjective "antadiform" does not appear in major dictionaries.
It is safe for learners to ignore words like "antadiform".

senses.definition: Plural of anta; small square posts or pillars at the ends of walls, often beside columns in classical buildings.
examples: The hall was lined with columns and antae.
The architect copied the antae from an ancient Greek temple.

senses.definition: Very strong or powerful, like the giant Antaeus in Greek myth; used mainly in literary style.
examples: He showed an Antaean strength during the crisis.
The team made an Antaean effort to finish the project on time.

senses.definition: A giant in Greek mythology who was very strong as long as he touched the earth, and was defeated by Hercules.
examples: In the story, Hercules kills Antaeus by lifting him off the ground.
Antaeus is often used as a symbol of strength connected to the earth.

senses.definition: Able to be antagonized or made hostile.
examples: In a tense situation, almost anyone is antagonisable.
Highly antagonisable audiences may react badly to criticism.

senses.definition: The act or process of making someone hostile or unfriendly.
examples: Constant antagonisation of the staff damaged morale.
The politician's speech led to the antagonisation of many voters.

senses.definition: Plural of antagonisation; different acts or processes of making people hostile or unfriendly.
examples: The report described several antagonisations of the local community.
These repeated antagonisations made cooperation impossible.

senses.definition: To make someone angry or unfriendly towards you.
examples: You should not antagonise your colleagues with unfair comments.
His rude remarks antagonised many people in the audience.

senses.definition: To act in opposition to something.
examples: Several groups chose to antagonise the new policy.
Local residents openly antagonised the project.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of antagonise: made someone angry or unfriendly.
examples: His comments antagonised the entire team.
The decision antagonised many loyal customers.

senses.definition: Third person singular of antagonise: makes someone angry or unfriendly.
examples: His aggressive style antagonises many colleagues.
The policy antagonises small business owners.

senses.definition: Present participle of antagonise: making someone angry or unfriendly.
examples: He kept antagonising his brother by teasing him.
Antagonising the public will damage the party's image.

senses.definition: Strong dislike or active opposition between people, groups, or ideas.
examples: There is clear antagonism between the two leaders.
Religious antagonism can lead to serious conflict.

senses.definition: In science, the effect when one substance or force reduces or opposes the effect of another.
examples: The drugs show clear antagonism when taken together.
Biologists studied the antagonism between the two hormones.

senses.definition: Plural of antagonism; different strong dislikes or conflicts between people, groups, or ideas.
examples: Old antagonisms resurfaced during the debate.
Social media can increase existing antagonisms in society.

senses.definition: A person who actively opposes someone or something.
examples: In the debate, he played the role of the main antagonist.
Every reformer faces antagonists who resist change.

senses.definition: In stories, the character who opposes the main character.
examples: The antagonist in the novel is complex and interesting.
In many films, the antagonist is not completely evil.

senses.definition: In medicine, a substance that blocks or reduces the effect of another substance.
examples: The doctor prescribed a receptor antagonist.
Scientists tested a new antagonist for the hormone.

senses.definition: Showing or feeling strong dislike or opposition towards someone or something.
examples: He is openly antagonistic towards the new policy.
The crowd became antagonistic after the announcement.

senses.definition: In science, acting against or reducing the effect of something else.
examples: The two drugs have antagonistic effects.
Researchers observed antagonistic interactions between the chemicals.

senses.definition: An old or very formal form of antagonistic, meaning hostile or strongly opposed.
examples: The letter was written in an antagonistical tone.
He remained antagonistical to any form of compromise.

senses.definition: In a hostile or strongly opposing way.
examples: He reacted antagonistically to every suggestion.
The two groups behaved antagonistically during the talks.

senses.definition: In science, in a way that reduces or opposes the effect of something else.
examples: These two chemicals work antagonistically in the body.
The drugs interact antagonistically at higher doses.

senses.definition: Plural of antagonist; people who actively oppose someone or something.
examples: His ideas had many outspoken antagonists.
The agreement was criticised by its antagonists.

senses.definition: In stories, characters who oppose the main character.
examples: The novel features several antagonists.
The antagonists in the film are not simple villains.

senses.definition: In medicine, substances that block or reduce the effect of other substances.
examples: Doctors tested several new receptor antagonists.
These antagonists reduce the effect of the hormone.

senses.definition: Able to be antagonized or made hostile.
examples: A highly antagonizable audience requires careful handling.
Children are easily antagonizable when they feel ignored.

senses.definition: The act or process of making someone hostile or unfriendly.
examples: Continual antagonization of the staff harmed the company.
The antagonization of local residents led to protests.

senses.definition: Plural of antagonization; different acts or processes of making people hostile or unfriendly.
examples: Repeated antagonizations eventually destroyed trust.
The report listed several antagonizations of minority groups.

senses.definition: To make someone angry or unfriendly towards you.
examples: His rude behaviour antagonized many voters.
You should not antagonize your customers with harsh words.

senses.definition: To act in opposition to something.
examples: Several groups decided to antagonize the new law.
Local leaders openly antagonized the proposed reforms.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of antagonize: made someone angry or unfriendly.
examples: His speech antagonized many people in the audience.
The decision antagonized long-time supporters.

senses.definition: A person or thing that antagonizes, making others angry or unfriendly.
examples: He was seen as the main antagonizer in the conflict.
Chronic antagonizers can damage a team's spirit.

senses.definition: Third person singular of antagonize: makes someone angry or unfriendly.
examples: His behaviour constantly antagonizes his coworkers.
The new policy antagonizes small business owners.

senses.definition: Present participle of antagonize: making someone angry or unfriendly.
examples: Stop antagonizing your brother with those comments.
Antagonizing the local community will harm the project.
